summary: An Islamic caliphate (750-1258) that overthrew the Umayyad Caliphate in the Abbasid Revolution and ruled from a newly founded round capital at Baghdad. Its rulers, including the fifth caliph Harun al-Rashid, presided over an Islamic Golden Age centered on the House of Wisdom, a mass translation institution for Greek texts, while also facing repeated internal revolts (the Zanj Rebellion, the Qarmatians), a period of military instability called the Anarchy at Samarra, and a clash with Tang dynasty China at the Battle of the Talas River. The dynasty nominally survived the rise of the Seljuks and Buyids before Hulagu Khan's Mongol forces sacked Baghdad in 1258, killing the last caliph al-Musta'sim by rolling him in a carpet.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: In giveaways this is consistently identified as the Baghdad-based Islamic caliphate that overthrew the Umayyads, ruled from 750 to 1258, and is associated with the House of Wisdom and its fifth caliph Harun al-Rashid. It is variously answered as "Abbasid Caliphate," "Abbasid dynasty," or "Abbasid Empire," and questions frequently require distinguishing it from its predecessor the Umayyad Caliphate and its rival the Fatimid Caliphate.

clues.clue: Baghdad-based caliphate/dynasty that overthrew or succeeded the Umayyads
examples: name this Baghdad-based caliphate that replaced the Umayyads.
name this caliphate based in Baghdad that overthrew the Umayyads.

clues.clue: Ruled from 750 to 1258 CE
examples: Name this Islamic dynasty that ruled from 750 to 1258.
This caliphate overtook the Umayyads in 750 CE.

clues.clue: Dynasty of Harun al-Rashid, centered at Baghdad
examples: name this dynasty of Harun al-Rashid, a caliphate centered at Baghdad which overthrew the Umayyads.

works.name: The Abbasid Revolution
works.description: The multi-year uprising (747-750) that overthrew the Umayyad Caliphate and installed the Abbasid dynasty in its place, sometimes clued as its own answerline separate from the caliphate itself. It began in Khurasan, where mawali converts alienated by harsh kharaj and jizya taxation rallied under the general Abu Muslim and the Black Standard; support also drew on decades of secret Hashimite organizing, including Hashimiyya Kaysanite missionaries called the "twelve naqibs." The revolution's decisive engagement was the Battle of the Zab, where Abu Muslim's forces defeated the last Umayyad caliph Marwan II, after which a surviving Umayyad prince fled toward North Africa (eventually founding a rival emirate). The Abbasid leader entered Kufa and took the title as-Saffah ("the Blood-Shedder"), and the word dawla shifted in meaning from "turn" to "dynasty" as a result.

clues.clue: Led by the general Abu Muslim under the Black Standard, based out of Khurasan/Merv
examples: Abu Muslim led an army under the Black Standard during what event, which saw the overthrow of the Umayyads?
Merv was captured during this uprising by an army under the Black Standard.

clues.clue: Decided at the Battle of the Zab, where Abu Muslim's forces defeated Marwan II
examples: Abu Muslim's victory over Marwan II at the Battle of Zab during this uprising enabled a dynasty to establish itself in newly-founded Baghdad.
This campaign ended with Abu Muslim's victory at the Battle of Zab.

clues.clue: Fueled by mawali converts angered by harsh kharaj and jizya taxes
examples: Support for this movement came from Khurasan, where new converts called mawali were alienated by harsh kharaj and jizya taxes.

clues.clue: Leader took the title as-Saffah upon entering Kufa; word dawla shifted meaning from "turn" to "dynasty" as a result
examples: A leader during this campaign granted himself the title as-Saffah upon entering Kufa.
Due to the outcome of this event, the word dawla shifted its meaning from "turn" to "dynasty."

clues.clue: Preceded by decades of secret Hashimite organizing, including Hashimiyya Kaysanite missionaries called the "twelve naqibs"
examples: On behalf of this dynasty, Hashimiyya Kaysanite missionaries known as the "twelve naqibs" led a secret organization to support their claim to the throne.

clues.clue: A losing Umayyad prince fled after horsemen left his decapitated brother's body to rot by a river
examples: After this multi-year campaign, a prince on the losing side supposedly ran for his life after horsemen left the decapitated body of his brother to rot near a river.

clues.clue: Started after a commander unfurled two flags called "The Shadow" and "The Clouds"
examples: This event reportedly started after a commander unfurled two flags called "The Shadow" and "The Clouds".

works.name: Early Revolts: Sunbadh and al-Muqanna "the Veiled One"
works.description: Shortly after the Abbasid Revolution, two uprisings challenged the new dynasty. The magus Sunbadh revolted after the execution of Abu Muslim, claiming Abu Muslim had become a dove in a brass fortress and, according to some sources, marching west to attack an institution. Later, a rebel known as "the Veiled One" (al-Muqanna) became known as "the abominable one" after winning the Day of the Barges, leading a rebellion of "red-wearing ones" (the Mubayyida) who held that the spirit of God existed in a general the dynasty had executed; a rebel in a related uprising severed a man's leg tendon with a trumpet before being made a commander.

clues.clue: Sunbadh (a magus) revolted after Abu Muslim's execution, claiming Abu Muslim became a dove in a brass fortress
examples: An executed leader of this event was said to have become a dove in a brass fortress according to Sunbadh, who some sources claim marched westwards to singlehandedly destroy one institution.
Shortly after its founding, this dynasty faced revolts led by the magi Sunpadh and a "veiled" general of a red-clad religious movement.

clues.clue: "The Veiled One" led the "red-wearing ones"/Mubayyida, who believed the spirit of God existed in an executed general
examples: The "Veiled One" led a rebellion of the "red-wearing ones" who believed that the spirit of God existed in a general executed by this dynasty.

clues.clue: A rebel became known as "the abominable one" after winning the Day of the Barges
examples: The leader of a rebellion against this dynasty became known as "the abominable one" after he won the Day of the Barges.

works.name: Harun al-Rashid and the Fall of the Barmakids
works.description: The dynasty's fifth caliph (r. 786-809), whose reign is remembered as a cultural high point. He founded the House of Wisdom and sent Charlemagne a series of extravagant gifts, including a water clock, ivory chessmen, and an elephant named Abul-Abbas. Early in his reign he relied heavily on the Barmakid family of Iranian viziers (descended from Buddhist leaders of Balkh, beginning with the vizier Khalid), who also patronized scholars such as Geber; Harun later destroyed the family's power after one of its members reportedly impregnated his sister Abbasa.

clues.clue: Sent Charlemagne a water clock, ivory chessmen, and an elephant named Abul-Abbas
examples: This fifth caliph of the Abbasid Empire founded the House of Wisdom. His court sent a number of extravagant gifts to Charlemagne, including a water clock and an elephant named Abul-Abbas.
This ruler sent Charlemagne a series of gifts, including an elephant named Abbul-Abbas, a water clock, perfume, and ivory chessmen.

clues.clue: Founded/established the House of Wisdom
examples: Identify this caliph who was advised by the Barmakid family. Among his more famous actions was creating the House of Wisdom in Baghdad.
This Abbasid caliph established the House of Wisdom and allegedly sent a water clock and an elephant to Charlemagne's court.

clues.clue: The dynasty's fifth caliph
examples: Harun Al-Rashid was the fifth caliph of this empire, which succeeded the Umayyad caliphate.
The Abbasids established this city as their capital... This fifth caliph...

clues.clue: The Barmakid family (Iranian viziers, descended from Buddhist leaders of Balkh, beginning with vizier Khalid) advised the dynasty early on
examples: The Barmakid family advised this dynasty early on.
'Arib claimed to have been born into this family of Iranian nobles, who were originally a group of Buddhist leaders in Balkh.

clues.clue: Harun destroyed the Barmakids' power after a member reportedly impregnated his sister Abbasa; the family had patronized scholars like Geber
examples: Harun also destroyed the power of this family after one of its members impregnated his sister Abassa. This family patronized scholars such as Geber.

works.name: The House of Wisdom and the Translation Movement
works.description: The Baghdad institution, built by al-Mansur and expanded by al-Ma'mun, that anchored the dynasty's mass translation of Greek texts and became the center of an Islamic Golden Age of scholarship. Scholars associated with it or working under Abbasid patronage include al-Khwarizmi (who wrote the Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing, developing early algebra), Ibn al-Haytham (author of the influential Book of Optics), a scholar who mathematically described what is now called Snell's Law in On Burning Mirrors and Lenses, al-Masudi (author of the geography Meadows of Gold and the Mines of Gems), the Brethren of Purity (whose 52 epistles formed an early proto-encyclopedia), and the three Banu Musa brothers, whose Book of Ingenious Devices (completed 850) described over a hundred automata, including a programmable flute player and a "haunted bottle" that dispensed two different liquids. A later peripatetic philosophical school arising under the dynasty led Aristotle to be called the "First Teacher" and the author of The Virtuous City the "Second Teacher."

clues.clue: Built by al-Mansur; center of mass translation of Greek texts into Arabic
examples: what Islamic state conducted mass translation of Greek texts at its House of Wisdom?
Nearly all of the aforementioned scholars worked in a structure built by Al-Mansur, the House of Wisdom.

clues.clue: al-Khwarizmi worked there / wrote the Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing (early algebra, quadratic formula)
examples: Among the members of the House of Wisdom is this early mathematician, who is probably most famous for deriving the quadratic formula and basically inventing algebra.
Mathematician al-Khwarizmi headed a central library known as the House of Wisdom in this dynasty's capital.

clues.clue: The Banu Musa brothers wrote the Book of Ingenious Devices, describing automata including a programmable flute player and a "haunted bottle"
examples: Three brothers serving this dynasty sketched many hydraulic automata in a Book of Ingenious Devices.
These brothers devised a "haunted bottle," into which two different liquids can be poured without mixing.

clues.clue: A scholar there produced the Book of Optics; another mathematically described Snell's Law in On Burning Mirrors and Lenses
examples: Another of its scholars first mathematically described Snell's Law in his book On Burning Mirrors and Lenses, while another book on light produced by this dynasty was the influential Book of Optics.

clues.clue: al-Masudi wrote the geography Meadows of Gold and the Mines of Gems under this dynasty
examples: One work of geography produced by a scholar working for this dynasty is called The Meadows of Gold and the Mines of Gems.
The collection Meadows of Gold, chronicling history from Adam and Eve to this dynasty, was written by al-Masudi.

clues.clue: The Brethren of Purity's 52 epistles formed the first proto-encyclopedia
examples: During its reign, a collection of 52 epistles written by the Brethren of Purity became the first published proto-encyclopedia.

clues.clue: A later peripatetic school led Aristotle to be called the "First Teacher" and al-Farabi (author of The Virtuous City) the "Second Teacher"
examples: Due to the influence of a latter-day peripatetic school that arose under this state, Aristotle was called the "First Teacher" and the author of The Virtuous City was called the "Second."

clues.clue: Secured a copy of Ptolemy's Almagest for the institution, which began as a library for either al-Mansur or al-Ma'mun
examples: Name this empire which secured a copy of Ptolemy's Almagest for an institution that began as a library for either Al-Mansur or Al-Ma'mun.

works.name: The Mihna and Mu'tazilite Controversy
works.description: A period of state-sponsored religious persecution, launched under caliph al-Ma'mun in support of the rationalist Mu'tazilite theology, which held that the Qur'an was created rather than co-eternal with God. Scholars who denied the Qur'an's createdness were persecuted at the mihna "inquisition" court. In a related episode of religious controversy, the mystic Mansur al-Hallaj drew the ire of an Abbasid ruler after uttering "I am the Truth."

clues.clue: Launched by al-Ma'mun to enforce Mu'tazilite doctrine that the Qur'an was created, not co-eternal with God
examples: This dynasty, established at Kufa, experienced a period of religious intolerance during the mihna inquisition under its leader al-Ma'mun.
Rulers of this empire persecuted scholars who denied the createdness of the Qur'an in the pro-Mu'tazilite mihna inquisition.

clues.clue: The mystic Mansur al-Hallaj angered a ruler of the dynasty after declaring "I am the Truth"
examples: The mystic Mansur al-Hallaj earned the ire of a leader of this empire after uttering "I am the Truth."

clues.clue: al-Tabari inaugurated a scholarly tradition of tafsir (Qur'anic exegesis) in the 9th century, during which the Mu'tazili school thrived
examples: al-Tabari inaugurated a scholarly tradition of tafsir in the 9th century during the reign of this caliphate, in which the Mu'tazili school thrived.

works.name: The Zanj Rebellion and the Qarmatians
works.description: The Zanj Rebellion (869-883) was a major revolt of enslaved Black plantation laborers ("Zanj," or "country of the blacks") in Basra and southern Iraq, led by Ali ibn Muhammad, who claimed descent from the fourth caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib; the Abbasid historian al-Tabari chronicled the rebellion in his History of the Prophets and Kings. A separate rebel, Rayhan, was among the revolt's leaders, and a related "anarchy" was partly ended by Musa ibn Bugha before a further revolt of salt-producing slaves. Decades later the Qarmatians, a syncretic Ismaili Shi'a sect that had set up a utopian state in Bahrain under Abu-Tahir Sulayman, openly revolted against the caliphate and sacked Mecca in 930, defiling the Zamzam Well with corpses and ransoming the Ka'aba's Black Stone back to the Abbasids for an enormous sum.

clues.clue: Zanj Rebellion: enslaved Black plantation laborers in Basra, led by Ali ibn Muhammad, who claimed descent from the caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib
examples: The Zaydi leader Ali ibn Muhammad led a revolt of Zanj slaves against this empire in Basra.
This dynasty was challenged by a revolt of black slaves known as the Zanj.

clues.clue: al-Tabari chronicled the Zanj Rebellion in his History of the Prophets and Kings
examples: This Abbasid historian chronicled the Zanj rebellion in his History of the Prophets and Kings.

clues.clue: Rayhan led part of the revolt; an "anarchy" was partly ended by Musa ibn Bugha, followed by a revolt of salt-producing slaves
examples: Rayhan was one of the leaders of a rebellion against this dynasty; that rebellion's name literally means "country of the blacks." An "anarchy" during the reign of this dynasty was partially ended by Musa ibn Bugha, and was followed by a revolt of salt-producing slaves.

clues.clue: Qarmatians (Ismaili Shi'a sect, utopian state in Bahrain under Abu-Tahir Sulayman) sacked Mecca in 930, defiled the Zamzam Well, and ransomed the Black Stone
examples: This group sacked Mecca in 930 and ransomed the Black Stone of the Ka'aba back to the Abbasids for an enormous amount.
The Qarmatians openly revolted against this caliphate, the first to establish its capital at Baghdad.

works.name: Samarra: the New Capital and the "Anarchy"
works.description: Al-Mu'tasim moved the Abbasid capital from Baghdad to Samarra in 836 CE after tensions between Turkic slave-soldiers (later called mamluks or ghilman) and Arab residents provoked riots. Al-Mutawakkil ruled from Samarra and built its Great Mosque, whose minaret has a distinctive spiral ramp, before his murder by his own Turkic bodyguards triggered a decade of instability known as the Anarchy at Samarra (861-870), which began with the death of al-Muntasir and saw al-Mu'taz eventually succeed his cousin al-Musta'in; the instability weakened the caliphate enough to allow Ahmad ibn Tulun's rise in Egypt.

clues.clue: Capital moved from Baghdad to Samarra in 836 after tensions between Turkic soldiers and Arab residents/Baghdad locals
examples: Tensions between Turkic and Arab soldiers led Al-Mu'tasim to move the Abbasid capital from Baghdad to this city in 836 CE.
After conflicts arose between residents of Baghdad and his Turkish slave-soldiers, Al-Mu'tasim founded this city up the Tigris in 836 to serve as a new capital.

clues.clue: Al-Mutawakkil built Samarra's Great Mosque (spiral-ramped minaret) and was later murdered by his own Turkic bodyguards, triggering the Anarchy
examples: Al-Mutawakkil ruled from this city, where he constructed a "Great Mosque" whose minarets have spiral ramps.
After trying to curb the growing power of his Turkic military commanders, this dynasty's ruler Al-Mutawakkil was murdered by his Turkic bodyguards.

clues.clue: Anarchy at Samarra began with the death of al-Muntasir; al-Mu'taz eventually succeeded his cousin al-Musta'in
examples: Name this period that began with the death of Al-Muntasir. This period eventually saw the succession of Al-Mu'taaz over his cousin Al-Musta'in.

clues.clue: The Anarchy weakened the caliphate enough to allow Ahmad ibn Tulun's rise in Egypt
examples: This government was weakened by the Anarchy at Samarra, which allowed for the rise of Ahmad ibn Tulun in Egypt.

clues.clue: Turkic military forces (mamluks/ghilman, purchased from Transoxiana) were used in wars with the Byzantine Empire under al-Ma'mun and al-Mu'tasim
examples: Name this dynasty whose rulers Al-Mamun and Al-Mu'tasim used Turkic military forces in their wars with the Byzantine Empire.

works.name: The Battle of Talas River and Relations with Tang China
works.description: The 751 Battle of Talas River (in modern Kyrgyzstan) pitted Abbasid forces against Tang dynasty China; the Abbasids won after Karluk mercenaries defected from the Tang general Gao Xianzhi (Goseonji) and after allied troops from the Ferghana Valley deserted the Chinese side. According to al-Tabari, the battle spread the secret of papermaking westward, leading to the first non-Chinese paper mill, at Samarqand. The two empires, whose capitals were the world's two most populous cities in 750 CE, otherwise had extensive contact: a shipwreck off Belitung island preserved evidence of maritime trade between them, the Abbasids sent mercenaries under Abu Jafar to help the Tang suppress the An Lushan Rebellion, and each power referred to emissaries of the other as "those with the black robes" because of their dress.

clues.clue: 751 Battle of Talas River: Abbasids defeated the Tang after Karluk mercenaries defected from Chinese general Gao Xianzhi (Goseonji)
examples: A battle between these two empires was decided by the defection of Karluk mercenaries from the side of general Goseonji to the other.
Modern-day Kyrgyzstan was also the site of the 751 Battle of Talas River, at which the Tang dynasty was beaten back by this Islamic caliphate.

clues.clue: The battle spread papermaking westward (per al-Tabari), leading to the first non-Chinese paper mill at Samarqand
examples: According to al-Tabari, a battle between these two empires led to the spread of paper-making to the west.
The first non-Chinese paper mill in the world sprung up in Samarqand after prisoners of war from one of these empires leaked the secret of papermaking to the other.

clues.clue: Abu Jafar sent Abbasid mercenaries to help the Tang suppress the An Lushan Rebellion
examples: name these two powers, one of which had a ruler named Abu Jafar who send the other some mercenaries to help put down the An Lushan rebellion.

clues.clue: In 750 CE the two empires' capitals were the world's two most populous cities
examples: In 750 AD, the capitals of these two empires were the two most populous cities in the world.

clues.clue: Each empire's emissaries were known to the other as "those with the black robes"
examples: Noting the black garments of officials and emissaries, people from one of these empires became known as "those with the black robes" to the other.

works.name: The Mongol Sack of Baghdad (1258)
works.description: Hulagu Khan, a grandson of Genghis Khan and later founder of the Ilkhanate, sacked Baghdad in 1258, destroying the House of Wisdom and, according to legend, turning the Tigris River black with ink from the destroyed library's manuscripts. The last Abbasid caliph, al-Musta'sim, was executed by being rolled in a carpet and trampled by horses. Hulagu's westward campaign was later halted by Mamluk forces at the Battle of Ain Jalut.

clues.clue: The last caliph al-Musta'sim was rolled in a carpet and trampled by horses
examples: It came to an end after its ruler Al-Musta'sim was rolled into a carpet in 1258.
This dynasty, which outlasted the Anarchy at Samarra, ended its rule when Hulegu Khan rolled its last ruler into a carpet and trampled him with horses.

clues.clue: Hulagu Khan (grandson of Genghis Khan, founder of the Ilkhanate) sacked Baghdad, destroying the House of Wisdom
examples: Many of the Abbasid achievements were reversed when this leader sacked Baghdad in 1258. This grandson of Genghis Khan founded the Ilkhanate.
The House of Wisdom was destroyed during this Mongol ruler's invasion of Baghdad.

clues.clue: The Tigris River ran black with ink from destroyed manuscripts
examples: The Tigris River turned black from ink after the House of Wisdom in this city was destroyed by the Mongols in 1258.
The sacking allegedly caused this city's rivers to run black with ink because of all the great scholarship that occurred here.

clues.clue: Hulagu's advance was later halted by the Mamluks at the Battle of Ain Jalut
examples: His conquests for the Ilkhanate in the Middle East were halted at the Battle of Ain Jalut.

works.name: The Qiyan: Enslaved Court Musicians
works.description: The qiyan were enslaved female singer-courtesans in Abbasid high society, roughly analogous to later Japanese geisha. The most famous was Arib al-Ma'muniya, who was purchased by the caliph al-Ma'mun (who also built the House of Wisdom), learned music in Basra (where the secret philosophical society the Brethren of Purity published musical theory), and defeated the qayna Shariya in a singing contest at Samarra. Arib claimed descent from the Barmakid family of Iranian viziers.

clues.clue: Arib al-Ma'muniya, purchased by al-Ma'mun, defeated the qayna Shariya in a singing contest at Samarra
examples: Arib destroyed the qayna Shariya in a music contest while in this city, which replaced Baghdad as the Abbasid capital under al-Mu'tasim.
'Arib was purchased by al-Ma'mun, a ruler of this Baghdad-based Islamic dynasty.

clues.clue: Qiyan were enslaved singer-courtesans roughly analogous to later Japanese geisha
examples: Name these performers who were roughly analogous to the later geisha of Japan. They served as both entertainers and courtesans while enslaved to Baghdad's elite.

comprehensive_summary: The Abbasid Caliphate (750-1258) was an Islamic dynasty that overthrew the Umayyad Caliphate in the Abbasid Revolution, a multi-year uprising rooted in Khurasan and led by the general Abu Muslim under the Black Standard, whose decisive victory over the last Umayyad caliph Marwan II at the Battle of the Zab allowed the new dynasty's leader, as-Saffah, to take power at Kufa. The dynasty founded a round capital at Baghdad and soon faced early revolts, including uprisings by the magus Sunbadh (avenging the execution of Abu Muslim) and by "the Veiled One," al-Muqanna. Its fifth caliph, Harun al-Rashid, presided over a cultural high point, founding the House of Wisdom, exchanging lavish gifts with Charlemagne (including an elephant, Abul-Abbas), and relying on the Barmakid family of viziers before destroying their power over a scandal involving his sister Abbasa. The House of Wisdom, built by al-Mansur and expanded by al-Ma'mun, anchored a mass translation of Greek texts and hosted scholars such as al-Khwarizmi (founder of algebra), Ibn al-Haytham (author of the Book of Optics), al-Masudi (author of the Meadows of Gold), the Brethren of Purity, and the Banu Musa brothers, whose Book of Ingenious Devices catalogued automata. Al-Ma'mun also launched the mihna, a period of persecution enforcing Mu'tazilite theology that the Qur'an was created. The dynasty weathered major revolts, including the Zanj Rebellion of enslaved plantation laborers in Basra led by Ali ibn Muhammad and the Qarmatian sack of Mecca in 930. Internal Turkic-Arab tensions led al-Mu'tasim to move the capital to Samarra in 836; the murder of al-Mutawakkil by his own Turkic bodyguards triggered the decade-long Anarchy at Samarra, which weakened central authority enough to allow Ahmad ibn Tulun's rise in Egypt. Abroad, the Abbasids clashed with Tang dynasty China at the 751 Battle of Talas River, a victory credited with spreading papermaking westward, while otherwise engaging in extensive trade and diplomatic contact with Tang China. Abbasid high society featured qiyan, enslaved singer-courtesans such as Arib al-Ma'muniya. The dynasty's power waned under the Buyids and Seljuks before Hulagu Khan's Mongol army sacked Baghdad in 1258, destroying the House of Wisdom and executing the last caliph, al-Musta'sim, by trampling him wrapped in a carpet.
recursive_suggestions: Harun al-Rashid -- the dynasty's most-clued ruler; could warrant a standalone History figure page
Abu Muslim -- leader of the Abbasid Revolution; recurring figure across multiple clue clusters
House of Wisdom -- recurring institutional answerline with its own dense cluster (al-Khwarizmi, Banu Musa, Book of Optics, etc.)
Zanj Rebellion -- appeared with enough independent detail to be its own answerline in some sets
Battle of Talas River -- clued both alone and paired with the Tang dynasty as a combined two-answer tossup
Qarmatians -- distinct Ismaili sect with its own dense clue cluster (Salamiyyah, Abu-Tahir Sulayman)
al-Khwarizmi -- mathematician with recurring individual clues; possible Science/Math crossover topic
Umayyad Caliphate -- predecessor dynasty overthrown in the Abbasid Revolution; distinct History topic
Fatimid Caliphate / al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah -- rival caliphate referenced tangentially (excluded here except where the Abbasids were the actual answer)
Ambiguity flag: several bonus/tossup questions required two answers (Abbasid Caliphate AND Tang dynasty); only the Abbasid-specific content from those was included here.
